name: Private Container Registry Governance
owner: TBD — accountable McCain architect not provided in attached context
approvers: TBD — governing board and named approver not provided in attached context
strategicIntent: This Draft pattern establishes McCain guardrails for private container registry governance across containers & kubernetes so teams can reuse a vetted design reference early in solution shaping. Missing items before endorsement: named McCain owner/approver, approved CCoE certification reference, LeanIX registration, and validated implementation asset links from the enterprise repository.
problemStatement: McCain container platforms need baseline pod, image, and traffic controls so Kubernetes workloads do not bypass enterprise security and observability standards. This pattern solves the specific design decision for private container registry governance.

useCases.use: Apply Private Container Registry Governance when a McCain project needs a reusable, governed approach in the CT domain for production or pre-production workloads.
useCases.dontUse: Do not use where a temporary proof of concept has no enterprise data, no plant connectivity, and no path to production without later architecture review.

useCases.use: Use during vendor solution assessment to compare proposed architecture against McCain guardrails and exception requirements.
useCases.dontUse: Do not use to justify bypassing domain-specific risk assessment, data classification, or OT safety review where those reviews are required.

components: Azure Kubernetes Service — managed Kubernetes runtime for containerised workloads
Azure Container Registry — private image registry and vulnerability scanning integration
Kubernetes Admission Policy — enforcement for pod security and approved images
Network Policy — namespace and pod-level traffic segmentation
Azure Monitor Container Insights — cluster and workload observability
interactionSteps: 1. Submit workload manifest for private container registry governance.
2. Validate image and pod policy for private container registry governance.
3. Admit workload to governed namespace for private container registry governance.
4. Control ingress/egress and observe runtime for private container registry governance.
5. Remediate drift or security findings for private container registry governance.

securityConsiderations: Access must use least privilege and be traceable to an approved McCain identity, workload identity, or support process.
Any PII, employee, supplier, customer, quality, or production-sensitive data must be classified before the pattern is implemented.
Administrative actions must be logged to the central observability/SOC platform with retention aligned to the applicable data class.

guardrails.rule: Production use requires private or controlled access paths; public exposure requires documented exception and compensating controls.
The workload must have owner, cost centre, data classification, environment, and criticality metadata before production approval.
Monitoring, alert routing, and operational runbook ownership must be defined before go-live.
Teams should define expected capacity, retention, and consumption limits during design review.

benefits: Gives McCain teams a common review reference for private container registry governance instead of re-solving the design per project.
Improves audit readiness by documenting required controls, evidence, and exception paths early.
risks: If private container registry governance is applied without data classification, criticality assessment, or domain review, teams may create false assurance and miss local plant or regulatory constraints.
constraints: Specific McCain tool URLs, CCoE-certified modules, named owners, and LeanIX references are TBD because they were not provided in the attached context.

exceptionProcess: Contact the McCain Enterprise Architecture Review Board and the relevant domain architect. Submit an Architecture Decision Record, data classification, threat model, operational impact assessment, compliance impact mapping, and compensating controls through the architecture governance intake; approval is required before implementation.
